title: Vendrix: platform maturity before acquisition
summary: Cloud platform, migration, and software-operations work behind a construction finance product later acquired by Foundation Software.

sections.title: Leadership
body: I managed one engineer while owning the v2 frontend and backend release path, coordinating product delivery across application work, partner integrations, and platform changes.
That work required direct prioritization across feature scope, architecture decisions, and operational readiness rather than treating platform and product work as separate tracks.

sections.title: Problem
body: Vendrix needed a stronger v2 release for construction finance workflows while the product and customer footprint were still growing.
The system had to support API and ERP integrations, a more expressive transaction model, repeatable deployments, and tighter security controls without slowing engineering down.

sections.title: Product and platform work
body: I designed partner and third-party ERP integrations around robust schemas, including a transaction-tagging model that let customers link spend to budgets, projects, resources, tools, and equipment.

I also owned GCP platform architecture across Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, Pub/Sub, Cloud Tasks, Scheduler, Cloud Storage events, IAM, Secret Manager, Cloud Build, and Cloud Monitoring, including migration and hardening work.

sections.title: Acquisition context
body: Foundation Software announced the acquisition of Vendrix, describing Vendrix as a construction financial management platform and emphasizing its software fit with the broader product suite.

My contribution was on the delivery and platform side of that fit: release ownership, integrations, schema design, deployability, security, migration work, logging, monitoring, and operational readiness.

title: Offline ops and edge telemetry
summary: An operations system that treats unreliable connectivity, device health, and alert quality as first-class design constraints.

sections.title: Problem
body: Operational data lived across paper receipts, messages, photos, and manual checks, while connectivity remained unreliable.
Critical well status needed faster detection and clearer escalation than human polling could provide.

sections.title: Architecture
body: The solution paired an offline-first PWA with local queues and background sync alongside a Raspberry Pi telemetry agent that buffered readings and sent signed heartbeat messages.
Cloud-side storage, indexing, and alerting were built with idempotent processing so reconnects and repeated sends did not corrupt the record.

sections.title: Outcome
body: Manual checks dropped by roughly 60 to 80 percent while outage detection improved from six to twelve hours to under ten minutes.
Alert routing rules for quiet hours, dedupe, and throttling kept operators focused on actionable issues instead of notification noise.

title: Real estate investment analytics on the same platform patterns
summary: Applied the same ingestion and governance approach to assessor-delivered spreadsheets, parcel analytics, and forecasting.

sections.title: Problem
body: Manual assessor-delivered spreadsheets and fragmented analytics slowed decision-making and created reporting defects.
The platform needed freshness, auditability, and an architecture that could tolerate ongoing feed variation.

sections.title: Implementation
body: The solution automated ingestion into curated warehouse models with validation, anomaly checks, schema drift handling, and replay and backfill support.
On top of the warehouse, the system added parcel-level geospatial analytics, alerting rules, and a forecasting MVP with backtesting and monitoring.

sections.title: Results
body: Data freshness improved from roughly one to three days after receipt to under fifteen minutes.
Forecasting backtests landed around 12 to 15 percent MAPE compared with an 18 to 22 percent baseline, while reporting defects dropped through automated validation.

title: Grounded knowledge with governance and evaluation
summary: A knowledge system built around metadata, citations, refusal behavior, and operational controls instead of trust-by-default generation.

sections.title: Problem
body: Important policy and procedure knowledge lived in documents, making answers inconsistent and lookup expensive.
The system needed to improve retrieval quality while maintaining confidentiality boundaries and clear auditability.

sections.title: Architecture
body: Documents are ingested with metadata such as classification tags and retention flags before being indexed for hybrid retrieval.
Responses require citations, refusal mode is enabled when evidence is weak, and evaluation regressions are tied to model or index changes.

sections.title: Controls
body: Matter-scoped or role-scoped access, encryption, audit logs, and restore drills are part of the operating model rather than add-ons.
This keeps the system useful for sensitive environments without turning it into an opaque black box.
